mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 318i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 318i engine typically costs £600–£4,500 supply only depending on which generation, with used units available from £500–£1,500 for older engines. Fitting adds £700–£2,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends entirely on which of the nine engine generations your 318i has. The M10/M40/M42/M43/M44 engines are robust but ageing. The N42/N46 Valvetronic engines introduced timing chain issues. The N43 direct-injection engines are the least reliable. The N13 turbo has a poor reputation for timing chain and oil consumption. The B38/B48 (2019+) are genuinely reliable. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 318i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M10/M40/M43: timing belt failure (requires regular replacement). M42/M44: generally reliable — check VANOS and cooling system. N42/N46: timing chain and VANOS issues. N43/N45: timing chain, injector and carbon build-up issues. N13: timing chain and oil consumption. B38/B48: oil filter housing and cooling system leaks. The B38/B48 is the most reliable generation.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 318i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept G20 318i worth £15,000+, a £2,500–£4,500 recon is only 15–30% — worth it. Against a tired £1,500 E30 318i, a £1,500 recon exceeds 70% — get a scrap valuation first. The value of the car matters more than the engine.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 318i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 318i has used nine distinct engine families. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 318i timing belt, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M10/M40/M43 engines, the timing belt must be replaced — it's a wear item. Replacement interval is 40,000–60,000 miles or 4–5 years. If the belt snaps, the engine is typically destroyed. On N42/N46/N43/N13 engines, the timing chain and tensioner need replacement if they show wear — preventative replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any 318i, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Belt Failure (M10, M40, M43)
problems.symptoms: Engine stops suddenly, loss of power, ticking from the front of the engine, complete engine failure

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the belt fails, the engine stops instantly and internal damage is highly likely

problems.recommendation: The M10, M40 and M43 engines use timing belts (not chains). BMW recommends replacement every 40,000–60,000 miles or 4–5 years. A snapped timing belt on these interference engines means valve and piston damage — effectively an engine replacement. If you're buying an older 318i with any of these engines, demand proof of timing belt replacement. Budget for it immediately if there's no evidence.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing belt failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain and VANOS Issues (N42/N46)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 2–5 seconds, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of low-end power (VANOS failure)
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,800 (timing chain, tensioner, guide and VANOS rebuild)
problems.replacementCost: £1,200–£2,200 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on N42/N46 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N42 and N46 Valvetronic engines introduced complexity with timing chain and VANOS issues. The chain guides can fail, and the VANOS solenoids and seals degrade. Cold-start rattle is the defining warning sign. A preventative chain and VANOS rebuild is cheaper than a full engine replacement. If buying an E46 318i, ensure the chain has been checked or budget for the work.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain and Injector Issues (N43/N45 Direct Injection)
problems.symptoms: Rattling on cold start, rough idle, misfire under load, vibration, engine warning light, poor fuel economy
problems.replacementCost: £1,500–£2,800 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on N43/N45 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N43 and N45 direct-injection engines are the least reliable of the 318i family. They suffer from timing chain issues and injector problems that cause vibration and rough running. Carbon build-up on intake valves is also a known issue. If buying an E90 318i, budget for timing chain work and injector cleaning or replacement. The N43 is widely considered the worst 318i engine in terms of reliability.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain and Oil Consumption (N13 Prince Turbo)
problems.symptoms: Rattling on cold start, oil level dropping between services, blue smoke on startup, rough idle, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (timing chain replacement and PCV system service)
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£3,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on N13 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N13 is a Prince-family engine derived from the PSA/BMW collaboration. It has a poor reputation for timing chain wear and oil consumption. BMW didn't formally recognise the timing chain issue in the UK/EU, so preventative replacement is essential. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. Check oil levels regularly — N13s are known for oil consumption.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 318i? Check These First
buyIf: * B38/B48-engined car (2019+) — the most reliable 318i generation by some margin
* M10/M40/M42/M43 with documented timing belt replacement — evidence of regular belt changes
* N42/N46/N43/N13 with documented timing chain work — the defining question on these engines
* Full service history with correct BMW-spec oil (varies by era)
avoidIf: * N42/N46/N43/N13 with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a known risk
* N43/N45 with vibration and rough idle — injector or timing chain issues incoming
* N13 with oil consumption issues — often indicates PCV or ring problems
* Any cold-start rattle on any era — this is almost always timing belt (older) or timing chain (newer) wear
* Poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 318i badge spans seven entirely different engine families over four decades. The early M10 and M40 engines are simple and robust but require timing belt attention. The M42/M44 twin-cam 16v engines are enthusiast favourites with excellent reliability. The N42/N46 Valvetronic engines introduced complexity and timing chain issues. The N43 direct-injection engines are the least reliable. The N13 Prince turbo has a poor reputation. The B38/B48 (2019+) are genuinely reliable modern engines. Buy on history, not on badge.

cta.label: → Compare 318i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 318i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Timing Belt History (M10/M40/M43) — Has it been changed within the last 40,000–60,000 miles?
2. Cold Start (all engines) — Any rattles? (timing belt or chain warning depending on era)
3. Service History — including which engine era and evidence of timing belt/chain work
4. Oil Level — Check for excessive consumption (N13 warning) or coolant contamination
5. Idle Quality — Any vibration or roughness? (injector or VANOS issues on later engines)
6. Coolant Level — Any loss? (cooling system issues on all generations)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
